title: BACK TO ONE
text: BACK TO ONE by Jessica McHugh T he Ninth Life Café is a gangrenous limb rotting the house to which it’s adjoined, and its owner is rotting with it. Every day her hip cat-adoption bistro remains closed due to the Covid-19 outbreak, Lisa Willis's heart withers a little more. She put up a hell of a fight to prove her small business wasn’t “non-essential,” but it was a fight she eventually lost. A lifestyle too. Not right away, of course. Pickup orders for her homemade muffins and sandwiches continued long enough to convince her she would triumph over the silly pandemic hoax. She even re-homed all but one foster cat after Ninth Life’s essentiality was called into question. But as the weeks in quarantine passed with no end in sight, pick-up orders petered out. Pop-ins became calls, calls became emails, and emails became empty platitudes like “hang in there!” in response to Facebook posts pleading for patronage. Hanging in there was no simple task. Lisa’s resources were already strained to their limit when someone dropped off a malnourished runt named Biscuit a few weeks after lockdown, and as the kitten devoured can after can of $2.35 gourmet sustainable organic tuna paté, she couldn’t help laughing at her bad luck. She does that a lot these days. Instead of facing reality, she pretends she’s the main character in a madcap sitcom, or the heel on a hidden camera prank show. Really hamming it up, she exaggerates every guffaw and eye roll and dances with the house-cats to riotous cheers from the audience in the shadows. Everything had been so unbelievably crazy for so long, it wasn’t difficult to convince herself she was being watched. Or that it was practice for all the interviews she’ll be doing once lockdown ended. She just needs to stick to the plan. Only the oldest house-cats remember how hard-won Lisa’s dream was. All those years planning and perfecting her vision for the cat cafe and proving to Charlie his money wasn’t going to waste—not that he cared all that much about money. He just wanted his young bride to be happy; the hows and whys of getting there didn’t matter. Frankly, that kind of attitude infuriated her. The path to achieving one’s dream was just as important as the dream itself, and every messy step deserved to be acknowledged, if not honored. Before they married, he warned her he wasn’t the type to get worked up about such things, even when he was in his prime, and after retirement, it was likely to get worse. But Lisa firmly believed the right woman could kindle even the lowest burning fire to a blaze. The right woman could also smother it. Charlie would’ve loved lockdown. The silence, the darkness. He was the kind of man who could stare out the window for hours, perfectly content, or read the same damn biography of Thomas Jefferson twice a year and still marvel at something new he’d learned. He didn't want dreams or aspirations or even hobbies that required much effort. Every day with breath in his lungs was enough for him, and having a beautiful dreamer for a wife to boot was, as he put it, “More than enough.” Lisa hated that. It sounded like a compliment, but it wasn't. If “enough” made him “perfectly content,” then “more than enough” had to feel overwhelming. “Don’t you ever get tired of me?” she asked more than once. “Don’t you ever wish I would stop asking you for things every second of every day?” He shrugged. “Not really. I like giving you things.” “But don’t you ever see what I’m doing and think, ‘Gosh, I could be out there helping people too. I could be making friends or traveling the world’. Don’t you ever get tired of just sitting around this house?” “Not really. I like this house. I like the cafe. And I love you.” “There must be something you’ve always dreamed of doing.” He smiled. “Your dreams are my dreams. And vice versa.” Even the memory made Lisa’s face tighten in irritation. Their dreams were never interchangeable. His were boring, rudderless things, like the mushrooms growing in the cracks of the cellar floor. Hers changed lives. On the surface, Ninth Life was a cute little cafe where someone could grab a homemade muffin while playing with foster cats, but at its core, the cafe connected folks who longed for companionship with animals that longed for anything . And how was Lisa Willis repaid for this valuable service? Squinting at the cobwebbed cafe, she hisses, “Non-essential.” A hushed gasp rises from the studio audience, and she limps back into the house. They’re getting harder to shake. Even when she’s not trying to pretend, she feels like she’s being watched. She’s never seen anyone looking back from the dark, but they’re real enough in her mind. Their eyes, gray lidless orbs, are sunken and off-set, like God plopped them into place while the flesh was still drying. And when they applaud her endeavors, success or failure, exposed bones clatter against one another, and lumps of withered flesh drop to the floor like overripe plums. Thankfully she's practical enough to recognize it’s just the stresses of quarantine playing tricks, so whenever visions of the dead audience frighten her, she throws her head back and chuckles uproariously for the camera. It is pretty funny how powerful her imagination can be. In reality, the only thing watching from the shadows is Frankie, the cat that refuses to purr. Her old tabby Cornelius wobbles into the foyer with a creaky mew and bumps his head on her shin. She scoops him up and presses her lips to his crown, giving him a squeaky kiss that summons more cats. She needs them badly right now, or she’ll never make it through the next step of the plan. Unfortunately the sound also calls Frankie, who hasn’t gotten the hint that her kisses aren’t for him. Not until he gives her what she needs. Cornelius mews again, this time in pain. She’s squeezing him too hard and he’s trying to escape. When she loosens her grip, he bounds out of her arms and gallops down the hall, past the grandfather clock, where Frankie sits, chin raised and tail slowly swishing. She hisses, “Non-essential,” and Frankie’s eyes widen. He sniffs the air, and as if mocking her with promises of fidelity, blinks in that sweet, slow way she associates with affection. It’s not real though. She’s lived with this mongrel long enough to know his blinks are a tease. Still, deep-down, from a place she can neither tap nor staunch, she feels a twinge of hope at every blink. She hates herself for that. It’s too familiar a cycle to repeat, even with a cat. Especially with a cat. Cats are supposed to understand you when humans don't. They’re supposed to love you unconditionally and make you feel like the cruelty and coldness of men can’t touch you. Cats and their people are supposed to keep each other safe, keep each other warm. They’re supposed to keep each other. Frankie's quick exit is sign enough he never got the memo. But when Lisa first saw him, abandoned on the shoulder of the highway, he walked into her open arms and rubbed his cheek against her chin in pure gratitude. From the start, he was a smiley gent with a swaggering sort of walk that made his poofy tail swish like a charmed snake. Playtime was always well-received, as was quiet time, for which he’d snuggle up in Lisa’s lap and softly knead her thighs. He ticked all the boxes right away...except for one. In all the weeks she spent with Frankie before putting him up for adoption at the cafe, all the nights he slept in a ball against the small of her back, she didn’t hear one rumble in his throat or huff of vibratory breath during one of his prolonged yawns. She’d known cats that weren't big purrers, but they were usually injured or traumatized in some way, and Frankie didn’t act like he’d experienced either. He was bold. He was sweet. He was very nearly perfect. Lisa didn’t know why it made her so angry. Or so depressed. But she took comfort in knowing she wasn’t alone in it. Frankie’s introductory Instagram post, which featured the large orange feline in a photoshopped top hat and bowtie, brought swarms of people into the cafe, squealing, “Where's the new one?” and “Lemme see that cutie you posted!” and “Please tell me you still have that big ginger boy.” Even with his defect, she thought it would be a matter of days, if not hours, before Frankie met his forever home. When he pranced over to show off the goods, people lost their minds. Even the most tolerant cat would’ve dashed away from a crowd of screaming humans with their wriggling fingers reaching out all at once, but not Frankie. He happily offered his chin and cheeks and fluffy buff tummy, and children begged their parents through torrential tears to let them take the kitty home. So many families wanted to spend time with him that Lisa scheduled a week’s worth of sessions the first day. But within minutes, the excitement every group had at the start of the session vanished. Despite all his other signs of affection, the cat’s refusal to purr upset them, even angered them. Several customers left abruptly, their sandwich half eaten and Frankie still standing with eyes closed and ears flattened in preparation for a head pat that would never come. Lisa still assumed he'd get adopted. How important was purring, anyway, especially when the cat had so much else to offer? He was a handsome creature, fluffy and smiley and cuddly: a real catch to anyone who didn't know better. But everyone knew better, and by the time the Governor forced her to shutter the cafe, Frankie was the only foster left. She tried to make the best of it. She invited him to be one of the venerated house-cats for as long as quarantine lasted. But the relationship couldn’t be one-sided. He had to pull his weight, and that meant loving her the way she needed. Using all manner of toys and catnip, blankets and brushes, she worked tirelessly to earn the cat’s love, like there was a magical combination to unlock his purr and make her mama to the greatest lap cat ever born. But it eventually became clear that Frankie didn’t want Lisa to earn his love. Try as she might, the cat simply wouldn’t purr. Not didn’t , she insisted. Wouldn’t . Spite grew thorns in the otherwise soft bouquet of Lisa’s savior complex, and the rage that followed shredded the pillowy blooms in a way she hadn't felt in years. Frustration, grief, and guilt, sure, but not spite. Frankie's many rejections brought it all back, like reopening old boils that leaked new ichor. The culmination of all her anger and jealousy colonized her once again and soon ran out of places to go. Escaping the confines of her body, it arched behind her like a stranger reading over her shoulder. It told her she wasn’t the problem and she was doing the right thing. How was she supposed to know she was bringing a cursed animal into her home? Squinting at Frankie she whispered. “Is that it? Are you cursed?” The cat stretched out his neck, his nose twitching, and just when she’d closed the distance between them, he released a massive sneeze that sprayed mucus across her hand, shirt, and face. Lisa growled through gritted teeth. The damn cat was cursed, and so was she. Come to think of it, she hadn’t heard anything about Covid-19 until after she rescued Frankie from the highway. Maybe she should’ve left him for the Fates and Ford Broncos to deal with. “This is all your fault,” she hissed. His whiskers splayed like she’d finally won his interest, but he abruptly spun around, giving her a perfect view of his puckered rear end. With an intensity she’d felt only once before, rage knotted up in her blood, causing explosions of pain and desperation that made her scream and stomp and close her hand around a mug boasting “World’s Best Cat Mom,” which she mercilessly hurled at her foster cat’s head. It landed short, the shattered porcelain sending Frankie skittering down the hall, but scaring him wasn’t enough. He needed to feel the terror and confusion she'd felt every day since he cursed the Ninth Life Café. The further she pursued him through the house, the further her sanity unhinged. She threw remotes and pillows and books and coasters, up and down the stairs and all the way to the kitchen where Frankie skittered out the flap on the door. She followed him out, screaming, “Good! Get out of here!” and he stopped, his tail swishing as he turned back to the house. “No one wants you! Just go!” she shouted, and his ears twitched as he started back toward her. She lifted her lawn chair overhead with a threatening squeal, and though Frankie continued to the house, he took a sharp turn and headed toward the cellar. She followed him, chair still overhead, and he picked up his pace, jogging out of sight. He was gone by the time she reached the small hole in the foundation, and she dropped the chair with a frustrated sigh. “You want to hang out with the worms and rot, fine. See if I care.” As she dragged the chair back to the house, the light shining from the kitchen filled her with sickening dread. She broke into a run, but not fast enough to stop Biscuit from dashing out the open door. Lisa screamed for him to stop, but the tiny cat scuttled joyfully out of reach and into the dark. He was still so small, so helpless. Frankie could take care of himself—not that she much cared either way—but Biscuit wouldn’t stand a chance against the coyotes that roamed the countryside at night. With every light on full blast, Lisa ran around the backyard, calling for Biscuit until her throat was raw. Hoping for as little as a mew in response, she shook treats and opened can after can of expensive tuna as she scoured the brush and tree line, kissing the air so many times her lips went numb. Her Facebook groups were no help, though she had to pretend they were so no one would know she still hadn’t found him eight hours later. Or heard a single cry. He was quiet for so long she thought maybe he’d started running and never stopped. But in the deepest part of night, when she’d passed out in her lawn chair and moths had come to settle on her arms, a strained yowl permeated her dreams. She hadn’t known Biscuit long, but she knew it was him calling for her. She searched the perimeter of the house and cafe again, then ventured into the woods and down to the stream, all the while shouting, whistling, and begging for some sign that Biscuit was okay. But the cry never got closer. It got more urgent though, strained and thin, and as the sky lightened with streaks of pink, the howl crescendoed to a scream that rivaled any she’d heard in the worst shelters. Back in the house, the sound continued with new zest. It felt like a living thing that hunkered down so heavily in her chest she couldn't take a full breath. She tried to sleep, but the yowling, which continued almost nonstop for the next three days, kept her from anything but shallow unconsciousness. It evolved too. Only a minuscule layer of the noise reminded her of a meow; the rest was an alien sort of howling and grinding that didn’t sound biological, let alone feline. The noise affected every creature in the house adjoined to the Ninth Life Café. After the first full day of screams, most of the house-cats stopped venturing below the second floor—many even took to hiding out in the attic where the noise was softest, leaving Lisa to suffer the torturous cries all alone. She played music at full volume throughout the house and cafe, but even the earsplitting soprano of Renee Fleming couldn’t dampen the kitten’s agonized cry. It felt like it was always just behind her, or underneath her, or inside her. She prodded her belly as if Biscuit was tangled up in her guts and laughed. The audience laughed too. They applauded as she spun in place, her hands in her hair and body aching with exhaustion. Charlie used to tell her all the time she needed to rest. “I don’t need rest. I need to keep my dream alive.” To which he’d nod and say, “Whatever you think is best.” But Lisa knew he didn’t believe it. He didn’t care what she thought or felt. He didn’t care what she said or did, just as long as she left him alone to be perfectly content with air in his lungs. Much good all that air did him now. That’s when she realized the answer to her problem. The only way to escape an unstoppable noise was through an untouchably deep sleep. And she knew just how to get it. Charlie’s liquor cabinet had gone largely ignored since he passed away—the cafe kept Lisa too busy for alcohol, even to relax. Charlie hadn’t been a big drinker, either, which annoyed her more than she knew it should have. In her experience, men with so few responsibilities, hobbies, or interests outside the home usually latched onto intoxicants to fill the hours, but not Charlie. He wasn’t even interesting enough to be a drunk. She quickly discovered she didn’t enjoy liquor, but seeing as enjoyment wasn’t the goal, she forced glass upon glass down her throat until, after what felt like days of binge drinking, a friendly oblivion dropped a rose-colored curtain over her eyes, and the kitten’s cries of agony stopped. Lisa’s began soon after. # She awoke broken at the bottom of the foyer stairs, soaked in blood and vomit. Everything hurt, every piece of her felt dislocated, but not as insistently as her left leg. Her vision was bleary, but she could tell her leg was bent the wrong way, and the colors were...off. She noted it casually, like when the cafe ran out of whole milk. Though an important issue, it didn’t seem as pressing as the small, mutilated creature standing in the hallway, licking its chops. From a ruptured eye, glistening sludge ran like a crusty river of black tears down its face, and each wobbly step caused a new lump of flesh to drop off its exposed ribs. The more meat dripping from its frame, the more the smell of eco-friendly tuna pâté filled the foyer. Her throat was the only part of her that felt dry, and it burned when she croaked, “Biscuit? Is that you?” Reaching for him sent pain rocketing through her body, and a new deluge of blood spurted from her leg. She hadn’t gotten a good look at the wound before, and she wished she hadn’t after. It resembled shredded deli ham soaked in merlot, and its run-off expanded in vast crimson-black puddles across the hardwood. Lisa’s brain swam with thoughts of calling for help and finding a tourniquet, but they quickly sank into an ocean of woozy ichor that swallowed her body, the house, and every part of her big dream. From that darkness something stirred. She thought it might be death coming to claim her, but it was worse. Smooth as a snake, even with leaves and dirt clods swinging from his body, Frankie strolled into the foyer. He paused to rub against Biscuit, who purred at the kindness, then looked to Lisa and blinked two perfect golden eyes. “Wait...how—” Frankie meowed and strolled through the sticky pools, tail swishing proudly. As he circled her body, the shadows shifted again, and what looked like gnarled twiggy fingers stretched across the floor and stroked his pointed head. He leaned into it, then followed its gesturing to the biggest puddle closing around Lisa’s disfigured calf. Terrified as she was, hope flared in her heart. Maybe Frankie was one of those pets that found a way to call 911. Maybe he’d just been waiting for the right time to reveal he was “Frankie the Rescue Cat,” a miracle in disguise sent to save her in such a spectacular way she’d wind up on the local news for the exact amount of time it took for her adorable cat cafe to become famous, finally giving her the kind of success no hoax could kill. With a prolonged meow, Frankie clawed at Lisa’s flesh, and her hope melted into the ichor. “Hey, get away! What are you—” Dizziness flooded her mind so suddenly she keeled over, landing with a smack on her right cheek. Her backwards leg was painfully visible at that angle, notably the spear of bone protruding from the mangled meat. Her clammy skin prickled, and she thought she might vomit again, but before her next inhalation, the pain was gone and everything was right with the world. Her body buzzed, her heart pumped a little stronger, and her vision cleared as the orange cat draped over her mutilated leg emitted a purr that sounded like every cat she'd ever met purring in unison. Each fiber of Frankie’s mane vibrated as his body grew heavier and wider on her leg, and the fever of infection bloomed into a different heat altogether, kindling a holy fire within every wound. Healing vibrations stampeded like cattle through her flesh. Her bones and tendons shifted beneath the cat’s body like her musculature was being unmade and reformed by his purr. It sucked the protruding bone back into place and glued it together with a pop of pressure, and endorphins rushed through her with a gusto she hadn’t felt since Ninth Life's opening day: a goulash of joy and doubt and desperation and at last, the affirmation that someone up there was looking out for her well-being...and her dreams. Nausea followed, but so did strength, and after a few minutes, she was able to pull herself to a seated position. She wasn’t as good as new, but there was a distinct newness to her—and the newness had memories. When Frankie’s purr faded and he crawled off her leg, scars remained like deep railroad tracks crisscrossing her flesh, but they didn’t inhibit her in any way. She wiggled her toes and bent her knee and marveled at the buttery fluidity of every motion. Despite the scars, it was like the fall never happened. It was also like she’d never twisted her ankle or sprained her toes in pointe shoes. She felt as strong as she was in her teens when her ballet troupe practiced every day after school and she thought nothing could stop her trajectory to the top. She jumped to her feet, and Frankie paused washing the blood from his whiskers. He cocked his head as she approached, whispering again, “What are you?” He flattened his ears, and with a prolonged squint, released a monumental sneeze that cleared most of the encroaching shadows. This time she didn’t flinch. She laughed and shook her head like he was the most precious creature in existence. And she hoped to hell he was. The ichorous swamp in her mind had dried up and ideas were able to take root again. Within minutes, what had started as a nugget of inspiration with “Frankie the Rescue Cat” became something much more valuable. He wasn’t just some handsome fella that refused to purr. He was a handsome fella who saved his purrs for when they were needed most. Purring was therapeutic, everyone knew that, but Frankie could heal bones and close wounds and, heck, maybe even cure Covid-19. Real or not, what did it matter once the cameras were rolling? Lisa felt like an idiot for judging him so harshly. Of course he couldn’t purr like ordinary cats. He wasn't an ordinary cat, just like she wasn't an ordinary woman. They had found each other to create beautiful miracles, not cuddle on the couch. Spotting her phone just a few steps up, an idea so big and bountiful formed within her that she felt like she was floating as she retrieved it. To create more beautiful miracles, she needed support, and to get support, she needed proof. Kissing air, she offered her hand to the misunderstood creature. “Come here, baby.” He stretched to sniff her fingers but didn't get within an inch before recoiling. His mouth opened slightly and his tongue twitched at her stench. Nausea and shame washed over her again and knotted into a troubling tumor of embarrassment as he walked away, as indifferent to her as ever. She seethed. She wept. Then, with phone in hand, she plotted. # “There have to be no other options.” Lisa confirms the cancellation of her cell service, salivating over her ingenuity. “I have to be helpless,” she says as she snips the cafe’s landline. “I have to be hopeless,” she says as she snips her car’s fuel line. It seemed so easy weeks ago. All she needed was a clean video of Frankie performing his miracle. But paper-cuts and bruised elbows weren’t going to do the job, she quickly discovered, and with her phone at the ready, Lisa ramped up her attempts at coaxing out Frankie’s magic. It wasn’t the first time she held a knife to her arm. Nor was it the first time she dared herself to press a little harder. It wasn’t even her first time with that particular knife. In hindsight, she should’ve known it wouldn’t work. Something she did to make Charlie understand the depths of her misery could never inspire something miraculous, especially since she hadn’t even wanted to get Charlie’s attention like that. Some people were just that dense. You could cry and scream and starve yourself, and they still wouldn’t see your suffering until you opened a vein on their church shoes. His face that day was a soft sheet of white like pizza dough hanging off his skull: an expression she's seen for years after the fact. But never straight-on. His face gapes in her periphery, sometimes so real she felt like he’s spent the last few years hiding in her blind spot. The blind spot is much wider now. As she pierced her forearm with the boning knife, as she shredded her fist punching through drywall, and as she held her arm over the stove-top flame so long her flesh bubbled and burst, leaving skin splatters on the burner, she saw Charlie, still wrapped up, sometimes dead, sometimes alive, but always laughing in time with the studio audience. Lisa did terrible things to others for the sake of her dream, and now it was her turn. But no matter how many slabs of flesh she sliced away, Frankie wouldn’t heal her. Sometimes he’d stand nearby, licking his ass while she bled. Sometimes he wouldn’t show up at all. But the shadowy audience was always there, applauding her gumption. And why wouldn’t they? What was skin or fingers or ears compared to the future bought with Frankie’s magic? His Instagram post had just been a picture and a few descriptors. Imagine the response to a video of the beast curing cancer. Now, as she waits for the cat to arrive, it's all she can think about. All the comments. All the shares. All the love. She fantasizes about popularity the way she fantasized about the cafe, knowing that manifestation requires a bit of obsession. It was no simple thing bringing a dream to life, let alone sustaining it. One had to surround themselves with like-minded dreamers in order to keep the passion alight. She had quite a bit of that during business hours, but once she was back in the house with Charlie’s dreamless aura, she felt a strange sort of doom strangling her. She couldn’t breathe sometimes when he was around. She tried to convince herself it was just anxiety, not a sign that sustaining was out of her grasp. After all, she’d done it well enough in other aspects: the lives of dozens of cats and house plants, for instance. She also had a knack for sustaining secrets. As did the shadows, some of which kept the same secrets as she, because they had no choice. If only she’d been a window he could look out in perfect contentment. But she’s so much more. She’s the glass and the landscape and the angry sky beyond it all. She’s the house too, the cafe and the dirt beneath, and every single mushroom sprouting in the cellar. Splayed in the foyer once again, kissing air and calling Frankie’s name, her gaze rolls over the ceiling: the tiny cracks Charlie always meant to fix, the flecks of gold contradicting them, the beam of rainbow sunlight bending over a dusty security camera— “Oh my God...” The audience claps, and Lisa’s breath catches in her throat, followed by a glut of blood she coughs up on her blouse. It rolls down her chest and joins the large bloodstain on her weeping stomach as she pushes herself up. Fighting the shutdown had occupied her so completely she’d neglected to turn off the security cameras when she closed down the cafe. If she was lucky, they’d been recording and downloading to her laptop all this time, months of the bistro and house from multiple angles, inside and out. All she had to do was find one good recording of Frankie's miracle. She doesn’t know which organ she’s holding inside as she struggles to stand. But it won’t matter soon. She just needs to get to her laptop upstairs. Something heavy and dark as shame pursues her through the house like an injured animal dragging its mangled legs. Though she never sees it, she smells where it's been, the stench of rot greeting her around every corner. Its shadow is more like an ink stain smeared on the wall than a kite tied to a living thing. It doesn’t move in time with the thing following her to the bedroom, but it crowds around her all the same as she opens her laptop and smears blood over the touchscreen. The cats crowd her too, yowling with a hunger for which Lisa deserves the blame. “Just a minute,” she tells them, breathless. “I just need to do this one thing and I’ll feed you.” They’ve heard it a lot over the last few weeks and no longer believe her. But she’s hungry too, and when they sniff her wounds, she doesn’t have the energy to wave them away. They lick and tug at the dangling meat as she scrolls through the videos. And though her strength is all but depleted, her eyes widen at the terrifying footage. With every camera angle open and playing on the screen, she realizes how many times Frankie had gone under the house, before and after Biscuit’s escape. She watches him heal the kitten after the coyotes got him. She watches him heal her leg after her drunken tumble down the stairs. She even watches him heal house-cats she hadn’t even realized had gotten hurt. But as captivating as it is, she finds herself focusing on the shadows instead. They’ve been healing too. He’s been healing. The first time she sees Charlie in the background of a video he’s little more than a tangle of crawling bones. But with each passing day, he sprouts more meat and muscle, he walks taller, and after months of visits from the magical cat at his burial spot, he reaches out to Lisa the way she reached out to Frankie: like a dreamer. The stairs creak, and she stops fighting the house-cats trying to work their snouts between her hand and her organs. They jerk her left and right, and she ultimately collapses backward onto the bed. Her bleary gaze fixes on the entrance to the bedroom, where Charlie’s shadow shrinks to meet him. As he nears the bed with Frankie purring loudly in his arms, Lisa’s heart quickens. He sets the magic cat on the bed and cradles Lisa’s face as he leans over her. Frankie draws close too, his purr like a volcano of promise, but he doesn’t touch her. Not a nudge, not a sniff. “Why?” she croaks, and the cat blinks at Charlie, who nods in understanding. With a dreamy grin, he kisses Lisa's crown and hisses, “Non-essential.” He pats his leg as he walks out, and Frankie prances dutifully to his side. Lisa hears him purring after the door shuts, but when Biscuit burrows into her belly like he’s curling up in warm laundry, it sounds more like applause. In many ways, Norman Prentiss hardly needs an introduction. As a two-time Bram Stoker Award Winner, Norman is one of the strongest, most consistent voices in quiet horror today, beautifully carrying forth the legacy set by such luminaries as Charles L. Grant, T. M. Wright, and Robert Aickman. His stories seem so normal, even mundane at first, because he hides the blade well, and his deft slash is so subtle, you won't even realize you've been cut until you start bleeding. I don't think there's a writer today who captures the horror of emotional turmoil better than Norman, but by all means, please don't take my word for it.
